normalized_episode: Cut Costs by Using Virtual Report Suites & Eliminating Secondary Server Calls Background: A server call, also known as a "hit" or an "image request", is an instance in which data is sent to Adobe servers to process. In this case, we are going to focus on two types of server calls: Primary Server Call: Requests received directly from website visitor browsers or the Data Insertion API. Includes Primary Hits (Page Views), Primary Custom Events, Primary Download Events, and Primary Exit Events. Secondary Server Call: Copies of primary server calls created by multi-suite tags or copied/moved by a VISTA rule. If a secondary server call has been moved (not copied) to a different report suite by a VISTA rule, the accumulated secondary calls are deducted from the primary server calls. Note: Adobe Analytics also tracks Mobile Primary Server Calls and Mobile Secondary Server Calls.   Solution: Identify if your organization is using Secondary Server Calls using Adobe Analytics Server Call Usage. Adobe Analytics Server Call Usage addresses your requests for transparency into both browser and mobile server call usage data. The main benefits include: Visibility into your server call consumption and commitment data, including mobile consumption vis a vis your contractual server call usage limit. Alerts to notify you of the risk or occurrence of an overage and prepare for/act on the possibility of incurring overages. Value: As an organization pays for Secondary Server Calls, eliminating them can result in significant savings. If your organization already uses a Global Report Suite that contains data, the relevant data is automatically will be in the new Virtual Report Suite. When you implement Analytics, if you use only a Global Report Suite with Virtual Report Suites, no secondary server calls are needed.   How to Identify Secondary Server Calls: Go to Analytics > Admin > Server Call Usage > Report Suite Usage. The Report Suite Usage dashboard: Shows the current usage period’s server call usage (All Calls, Primary, Secondary, Mobile Primary, Mobile Secondary) for each report suite in your Experience Cloud organization. Shows percentage of overall usage per server call category. Is updated daily. In the Secondary Calls column, see if they have any secondary server call volume. Secondary server calls should not be present. Identify the report suites with secondary server calls and compare it to your contractual limit. (Analytics > Admin > Server Call Usage) Identify report suites with secondary server calls and set up alerts to prevent overages. (Analytics > Components >Alerts) Background: A server call, also known as a "hit" or an "image request", is an instance in which data is sent to Adobe servers to process. In this case, we are going to focus on two types of server calls: Primary Server Call: Requests received directly from website visitor browsers or the Data Insertion API. Value: As an organization pays for Secondary Server Calls, eliminating them can result in significant savings. When you implement Analytics, if you use only a Global Report Suite with Virtual Report Suites, no secondary server calls are needed.   How to Identify Secondary Server Calls: Go to Analytics > Admin > Server Call Usage > Report Suite Usage. The Report Suite Usage dashboard: Shows the current usage period’s server call usage (All Calls, Primary, Secondary, Mobile Primary, Mobile Secondary) for each report suite in your Experience Cloud organization. Shows percentage of overall usage per server call category. Is updated daily. In the Secondary Calls column, see if they have any secondary server call volume. Secondary server calls should not be present. Identify the report suites with secondary server calls and compare it to your contractual limit. normalized_episode: Tag Manager for Managing & Organizing AA Tags Tag Manager in Adobe Analytics Labs helps users quickly find, compare, and manage tagged components. Tag Manager is accessible by navigating to Adobe Analytics > Labs.  Organizing and managing vast amounts of data is crucial for organizations as they scale analytics across their businesses, as users must stay up-to-date on tag management. The inability to manage tags causes roadblocks in supporting users to efficiently find, compare, or derive actionable decisions from metrics.  As a result, Adobe Analytics Labs offers a new feature called “Tag Manager.” Tag Manager enables admin users to view, edit, and delete tags while also providing users with tools that support filtering and exploring tagged components. In Analysis Workspace, users can search and filter tags in the left rail using the subsequent sequence: “tag: <tagName>”.  Using the tools in Tag Manager, admins can more effectively edit tags across report suites while also being able to clean up outdated or unused tags across their implementation. Furthermore, admins can use the feature to understand who created specific tags to communicate before any changes or deletions. Furthermore, admins can use the feature to understand who created specific tags to communicate before any changes or deletions.

normalized_episode: Export Workspace Projects to PowerPoint using Adobe Analytics Labs While Analysis Workspace is excellent for sharing data stories across Adobe Analytics users, users often need help if there is a need to share the report with outside individuals. Using the Workspace Project PowerPoint Exporter, you can go beyond exporting a project as a .pdf or .csv. You can automatically export visualizations and tables into a PowerPoint deck using the tool. As screenshots of the report and .pdf/.csv versions are a common way to share insights with a broader team, there are limitations around editing and clarifying key data points. This limitation may be a significant challenge if you want to emphasize or update critical findings from the analysis. However, using the PowerPoint Exporter tool, Freeform tables, including those with breakdowns, are transformed into native PowerPoint tables, making them easily editable and customizable. How to Use: Open the existing project or create a new one in the PowerPoint Exporter. Ensure the project is fully loaded and navigate to Project > Download PowerPoint.       3. Save the presentation to a location of your choice.  Upon opening the PowerPoint deck, you will see that it contains all your project's panels, data tables, and visualizations. However, please note that specific visualizations, such as Flow, Fallout, and Combo, as well as specific chart settings, such as dual-axis, are not supported in PowerPoint Exporter.            While Analysis Workspace is excellent for sharing data stories across Adobe Analytics users, users often need help if there is a need to share the report with outside individuals. This limitation may be a significant challenge if you want to emphasize or update critical findings from the analysis. However, using the PowerPoint Exporter tool, Freeform tables, including those with breakdowns, are transformed into native PowerPoint tables, making them easily editable and customizable. How to Use: Open the existing project or create a new one in the PowerPoint Exporter. Ensure the project is fully loaded and navigate to Project > Download PowerPoint.       3. Save the presentation to a location of your choice.  Upon opening the PowerPoint deck, you will see that it contains all your project's panels, data tables, and visualizations. However, please note that specific visualizations, such as Flow, Fallout, and Combo, as well as specific chart settings, such as dual-axis, are not supported in PowerPoint Exporter.

normalized_episode: However, remember that Low-Traffic values can still appear using Data Warehouse in specific scenarios. Low-Traffic values may still occur for list vars, list props, merchandising eVars, and marketing channel detail dimensions. Identify Low-Traffic Values in Data Warehouse Navigate to Adobe Analytics > Tools > Data Warehouse. Create a name for the report under "Request Name." Using either the custom or preset date range option, select the date range you used in Workspace to identify the Low-Traffic dimension item.  As the screenshot above includes a segment, you will want to do the same in Data Warehouse if applicable to your use case. Under "Available Segments," search for the segment used in your Freeform table. You can skip this step if you see the Low-Traffic dimension item without including a segment. Navigate to "Breakdowns" on the left side of the screen. This section is where you will select the dimension item to measure. In this case, we will choose Page URL as our dimension, as shown below:      6. Navigate to "Metrics" below "Breakdowns." This section is where you will select the metric needed to measure the dimension item. In this case, we will choose Page URL instances as our metric, as shown below:     7. Enter the email address where you'd like to send the report and hit "Request this Report." Once you receive the Data Warehouse report via email, you can view all Page URLs for the given date range without any Low-Traffic dimension values.

normalized_episode: @Gradsta Hi! thanks for reporting. My understanding is your use JINJA template to filter time column first and expect that becomes native filter on dashboard? @junlincc I would phrase it this way: I'm trying to use JINJA to dynamically populate values for a native filter based on a dashboard's selected time range. Expanding on my example above, the time filter on the dashboard is `Last year` which should resolve to `2020 <= order_date < 2021`. For the product line filter, I only want to display values that are applicable to the selected time range. In other words, populate the filter with the distinct set of product lines that correspond to vehicle sales that have an order date between 2020 and 2021. The query I wrote for the example was intended to more clearly highlight the issue but a more realistic query for the product lines filter might look something like this: ``` SELECT DISTINCT product_line FROM "Vehicle Sales" WHERE order_date >= {{ from_dttm}} AND order_date < {{ to_dttm }} ``` And just to reiterate, the problem is that `from_dttm` is resolving to `None` instead of a timestamp. `to_dttm` resolves to a timestamp as expected. Hopefully this clarifies things! Let me know if you have any other questions. Same issue, +1 I did find a work-a-round to at least get some values to populate: check "pre filter available values" and use a time range (probably the default for your dash). Same issue. In my case I would like to let users select also 'No filter' as a possibility in the Time filter box, so @rubypollev work-around is not useful for this case. @Gradsta you can solve the issue by using the following check: ``` {% if from_dttm is not none %} WHERE order_date > '{{ from_dttm }}' {% endif %} ``` I had the same issue as the gentleman with "none" being passed through to the query. The solution on the Filter panel on the dashboard was to go to "SCOPING" tab and ensure i had ticked the right 'chart' This issue has been automatically marked as stale because it has not had recent activity. It will be closed if no further activity occurs. Thank you for your contributions. For admin, please label this issue `.pinned` to prevent stale bot from closing the issue. I am having the same issue in 2.0.0, although my use case is different. I am displaying a report via a table-valued function like so: ``` {% set operatorId = 'DEFAULT' %} {% if filter_values('operatorId')|length == 1 %} {% set operatorId = filter_values('operatorId')[0] %} {% endif %} SELECT * FROM [dbo].[myFunction]({{ operatorId }}, '{{ from_dttm }}', '{{ to_dttm }}') ``` The report runs fine, but when I try to create a filter for operatorId, I get the " bad request" error, like in the image above. It seems like the filter runs a version of the same query in the background to get its values, but there is no value for from_dttm, so it breaks. I can partially fix this by setting defaults for "from" and "to" datetimes like this: ``` {% set fromDttm = '1753-01-01 00:00:00' %} {% set toDttm = '9999-12-31 23:59:59.997' %} {% if from_dttm is not none %} {% set fromDttm = from_dttm %} {% endif %} {% if to_dttm is not none %} {% set toDttm = to_dttm %} {% endif %} SELECT * FROM [dbo].[myFunction]({{ operatorId }}, '{{ fromDttm }}', '{{ toDttm }}') ``` Although, this is not ideal, because the operatorId filter will have values that occur outside of the user's given time range. EDIT: This also exposes a possible bug. I thought that would be better discussed in it's own issue: #22893 I found working solution of the problem "valid" at least for version: 2.1.0rc3. See picture below ... For my filter (called parity) I manually added dependency on time range filter. System is detecting dependency as invalid or deleted - as one can see on the picture - but it is working. ![sup](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/579632/229114088-fc263ffc-75e2-45a7-958c-33641a261e1d.jpg) The following image shows how to add a reference manually: ![sup2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/579632/229115110-7174b5b1-b9cc-4c41-b981-d3e579e03f4b.jpg) May be, the right solution of the problem is to make time range filter type as valid type for dependent filters. Also, filters that have dependency on other filters should be reset, when dependent filter changes.

Thanks. Hello @noobyogi0010 that would be great! Let me know if you are still interested in working on this issue @noobyogi0010 @geido Our customer also has issues with the combination of server pagination & csv export. The behaviour is different from exporting from a chart without server pagination. In a "normal" chart, all pages would be downloaded (e.g. 10 pages a 10 entries = 100 row csv). In a "server paged" chart, the "export CSV" function will - download the first page only - ignore changes to "show n entries" (always use the default "server page length") I would suggest not necessarily going @pratikgera123's proposed way (download the current instead of the first page) but instead harmonizing "server paged" CSV export with normal CSV export, e.g. they should behave the same. The user of a dashboard does not necessarily even know what server paging is and whether it's used, so they expect csv to behave the same either way. We had to suggest to our users to go via "view chart in explore", disable server pagination, and download from there. Not the best user experience, especially given that we have quite some users. Ideally, the CSV export option should offer a pop-up where such things can be selected before downloading ("download all pages" vs. "download current page", override for limit, etc.). While downloading the CSV file irrespective of pagination i.e. 10/20/50 items or "All" items in the list, the downloaded file consists first 10/20 records as they were set for pagination for display. Validated on multiple reports and different datasets. Expected - "Export CSV" option should be extracting records displayed on the view with respect to no. of records set to display on single page "Export full CSV" option should extract all records. Actual - Fixed number of records are extracted from both the options . > When multiple pages are present in a report and user clicks on export CSV , only the data from first page is getting exported in CSV even when any other page is selected. > > #### How to reproduce the bug > 1. Open any report > 2. Navigate to any other page using pagination > 3. Click on export CSV > > ### Expected results- Csv file with entries of current page should get downloaded > ### Actual results- CSV file with entries of first page getting downloaded > #### Screenshots > ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/96576388/147241095-d2681661-af93-4f40-9571-d5219aecd05e.png) > > ### Environment > * browser :Chrome > * superset version: `1.3.1` I am also getting the same issue. normalized_episode: Possibly related to #44329 Also have problem exporting/importing dashboards between Grafanas (even 8.3.4). Comparing old/new exports: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/50887992/150821257-a60863e1-4eeb-440d-90e0-c2c354da3036.png) Now datasources should have same id in all Grafana instances for dashboard to work? We've written a workaround script that goes through all dashboards, determines datasource variable usage, and then copies into every relevant panel target ```json "datasource":{"type": "prometheus","uid":"$the_variable_name"} ``` ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1084441/151889770-13e70f5c-a880-4314-ad91-b94dc67770a9.png) @william-tran can you share your script please? ```python ''' ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ Copyright (C) 2022 Autonomic, LLC - All rights reserved Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License"); you may not use this file except in compliance with the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0 Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS, WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied. See the License for the specific language governing permissions and limitations under the License. ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ ''' from curses import panel import json import os import sys import requests import traceback from typing import Any, Dict, List, Tuple def get_dashboards(api_key: str, url: str) -> List[Tuple[str, str]]: """Returns the current list of dashboards by its name and uid""" headers = {'Authorization': 'Bearer '+ api_key} response = requests.get(url + '/api/search', headers=headers) if response.status_code != 200: response.raise_for_status() dashboards = response.json() return [(dashboard['url'].split('/')[-1], dashboard['uid']) for dashboard in dashboards] def get_dashboard_content(api_key: str, url: str, uid: str) -> Dict[str, Any]: """Returns the content of a dashboard""" headers = {'Authorization': 'Bearer '+ api_key} response = requests.get(url + '/api/dashboards/uid/' + uid, headers=headers) if response.status_code != 200: response.raise_for_status() dashboard = response.json() return dashboard['dashboard'] def post_dashboard_content(api_key: str, url: str, content: Dict[str, Any]) -> None: """Uploads the content to a dashboard""" headers = {'Authorization': 'Bearer '+ api_key, 'Content-Type' : 'application/json'} data = {'dashboard': content, 'message': 'fix datasource variable script'} response = requests.post(url + '/api/dashboards/db', headers=headers, data=json.dumps(data)) if response.status_code != 200: response.raise_for_status() def get_variable_name(var: str) -> str: """ Returns the variable name from an variable expression Examples: '$env' => 'env', '${datasource}' => 'datasource' """ return var[2:-1] if var[1] == '{' else var[1:] def get_variable(var_list: List[Dict], name: str) -> Dict: """Returns the given variable definition from a list of variables""" result = list(filter(lambda x: x['name'] == name, var_list)) return result[0] if len(result) > 0 else None def update_panel_variable(panels: List[Dict], vars: List[Dict]) -> bool: """Updates the variable datasources for a given panel""" def contains_valid_datasource(datasource: Any) -> bool: """Returns true if datasource is using a variable, and false otherwise""" return isinstance(datasource, str) and len(datasource) > 1 and datasource[0] == '$' updated = False for panel in panels: # Traverse to the leaf panel if 'panels' in panel: updated = update_panel_variable(panel['panels'], vars) or updated continue # No need to proceed if the datasource does not exist or is hard coded if 'datasource' not in panel or not contains_valid_datasource(panel['datasource']): continue # Return the variable of the datasource variable = get_variable(vars, get_variable_name(panel['datasource'])) if variable is None: continue # Update the datasource of the panel new_datasource = {'type': variable['query'], 'uid': panel['datasource']} panel['datasource'] = new_datasource updated = True # Update the targets with new datasource if 'targets' not in panel: continue for target in panel['targets']: target['datasource'] = new_datasource updated = True return updated def main(argv: List[str]) -> None: if len(argv) != 2: print('Usage: add_datasource_to-dashboard_targets [grafana api-key] [grafana url]') sys.exit(1) try: for name, uid in get_dashboards(argv[0], argv[1]): print('getting '+name+'-'+uid) content = get_dashboard_content(argv[0], argv[1], uid) if 'panels' in content: if update_panel_variable(content['panels'], content['templating']['list']): print('updating '+name+'-'+uid) post_dashboard_content(argv[0], argv[1], content) except Exception as e: print(e) print(traceback.format_exc()) sys.exit(1) if __name__ == '__main__': main(sys.argv[1:]) ``` courtesy of @bosen-au. normalized_episode: Dashboard Section Tile / Div Equivalent? Hi all! Still relatively green in the Looker space, but as I’ve been working in Looker, I’ve realized I might be missing (or there might be an opportunity for) a really simple quality of life improvement in the dashboard editor. I’ve been working on some extensive, highly repetitive dashboards lately which involve copying the same 6-8 views and customizing them for different KPIs. Because of the collaborative nature of how we handle dashboard development in my org, I’m realizing that repetitively reorganizing this dashboard will be unduly time consuming. This made me think back to my web dev days and realize that I really wish dashboards had a tile type equivalent to a div block that could hold multiple tiles for easy rearranging and copy/pasting (i.e. an empty block that could hold several views and a text block and move as if it were a single tile instead of rearranging one at a time and potentially losing or grabbing the wrong view in the shuffle). Probably too simple a problem to be worth real attention, but I’m curious if others have had the same thought. Has anyone perhaps found a Block that accomplishes this? There seems to be some level of interest in a similar feature here , though the focus seems less on building and more on export controls. It’s also 3 years old, so maybe there’s more interest/need/nuance now. Hi all! normalized_episode: Hierarchial filters to load looks Hello Commnunity, I am looking to create something similar to hierarchial slicer on powerbi. In my case i have 10 levels of employees starting from level 1 (CEO) for 200 different companies. So my vision is that when i launch the dashboard the default filter would be list of 200 different CEO and then when i click on a particular ceo, everyone reporting directly to him are visible in the expansion with each value having a futher scope of expansion till level 10. For this i have two fields, one is employee id and other is manager id. manager id for CEO would be null and hence they are identified as CEO, all other employee id will have a manager id. My selection is going to be employee id which will populate all the measure realted to that employee. Hello Commnunity, I am looking to create something similar to hierarchial slicer on powerbi. normalized_episode: How to Track Persona Hi @ATiwari246 , In the free tools and Starter subscriptions, you could create one contact-list per persona, filtering for the persona (e.g. persona A) and add a filter for "Last seen" is known. This would then populate with all contacts matching the specified persona who have ever viewed a web page (after they've accepted cookies and submitted a form or clicked through a marketing email – HubSpot cannot track unidentified visits). Interactions with social media posts are not captured in HubSpot and it's not possible to filter for that directly. You can however check if Latest source has ever been any of Organic social: https://knowledge.hubspot.com/properties/understand-source-properties – it's not the same as post shares / likes / comments but would return contacts that converted after interacting with social media content. (Assuming that you schedule social posts through HubSpot.) If you're on a Professional or Enterprise subscription, you can use the same logic as above to create a report: https://knowledge.hubspot.com/reports/create-custom-reports Let me know if I should specify anything. Hope this helps! Hi @ATiwari246 , In the free tools and Starter subscriptions, you could create one contact-list per persona, filtering for the persona (e.g. persona A) and add a filter for "Last seen" is known. This would then populate with all contacts matching the specified persona who have ever viewed a web page (after they've accepted cookies and submitted a form or clicked through a marketing email – HubSpot cannot track unidentified visits). Interactions with social media posts are not captured in HubSpot and it's not possible to filter for that directly. You can however check if Latest source has ever been any of Organic social: https://knowledge.hubspot.com/properties/understand-source-properties – it's not the same as post shares / likes / comments but would return contacts that converted after interacting with social media content. (Assuming that you schedule social posts through HubSpot.) If you're on a Professional or Enterprise subscription, you can use the same logic as above to create a report: https://knowledge.hubspot.com/reports/create-custom-reports Let me know if I should specify anything. Hope this helps!
